Part 1: Complex Analysis

We begin reviewing some basic results in complex analysis. These properties will be useful when understanding the Laplace transform and Fourier transform, which will be discussed later. However, the most important thing to learn in this part is about integration in the complex plane.

Holomorphic Functions

A function f:C→Cf:\mathbb{C}\to \mathbb{C}f:C→C﻿ is complex differentiable, or holomorphic, at z∈Cz\in\mathbb{C}z∈C﻿ if

exists. The following are some of the properties of holomorphic functions.

Cauchy-Riemann Equations

Suppose that fff﻿ is holomorphic, and

Then, the functions u,v:R2→Ru,v:\mathbb{R}^2\to \mathbb{R}u,v:R2→R﻿ must satisfy the Cauchy-Riemann differential equations,

In addition, we define

then

Cauchy’s Theorem

Let Ω⊂C\Omega\sub\mathbb{C}Ω⊂C﻿ be an open set, fff﻿ holomorphic on Ω\OmegaΩ﻿ , and C⊂Ω\mathcal{C}\sub \OmegaC⊂Ω﻿ be an oriented smooth curve. We define the integral of fff﻿ along C\mathcal{C}C﻿ by

where γ:I→C\gamma:I\to \mathcal{C}γ:I→C﻿ is a parametrization.

One of the most important properties of a holomorphic function is known as Cauchy’s theorem.

If fff﻿ is holomorphic in a disc, then,

for any closed curve C\mathcal{C}C﻿ in that disc.

With these properties, we can actually calculate many integrals along the real axis by extending it into an integration along a contour in the complex plane. The situation that we integrate over a semi-circle of radius RRR﻿ and want that part to vanish is quite common, and Jordan’s lemma presents a useful general result.

It then follows that holomorphic functions are analytic, specifically, for all z∈Dz\in Dz∈D﻿

Residue Calculus

If f:Ω→Cf:\Omega\to\mathbb{C}f:Ω→C﻿ has a pole of order nnn﻿ at z0∈Ωz_0\in \Omegaz0​∈Ω﻿, then there exists a neighborhood U⊂ΩU\sub\OmegaU⊂Ω﻿ of z0z_0z0​﻿, numbers a−n,…,a−1∈Ca_{-n},\dots, a_{-1}\in\mathbb{C}a−n​,…,a−1​∈C﻿ and a holomorphic function G:U→CG: U\to \mathbb{C}G:U→C﻿ such that

The coefficient a−1a_{-1}a−1​﻿ of (z−z0)−1\left(z-z_0\right)^{-1}(z−z0​)−1﻿ is called the residue of fff﻿ at the pole z0z_0z0​﻿, denoted as

If fff﻿ has a pole of order nnn﻿ at z0z_0z0​﻿, then

Residues are very useful at calculating contour integration in the complex plane.

Part 2: Laplace Transform

The Laplace transform was first introduced by Pierre-Simon, Marquis de Laplace in his work on probability theory. The transform turns out to have many applications in science and engineering, especially in solving linear differential equations.

The Laplace Transform

Let f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿ be defined for 0≤t<∞0\leq t<\infty0≤t<∞﻿. The Laplace transform of f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿, which is denoted by F(s)F(s)F(s)﻿, or L{f(t)}\mathcal{L}\left\{f(t)\right\}L{f(t)}﻿ is given by

where the improper integral is understood as

Remarks:

Two conditions that fff﻿ should satisfy to have a Laplace transform (for sufficiently large sss﻿) are

  1. The function f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿ is of exponential order, i.e., there exist constants MMM﻿ and ccc﻿ such that for all 0≤t<∞0\le t<\infty0≤t<∞﻿,
  1. The function f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿ is piecewise continuous, i.e. f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿ has at most a finite number of discontinuities on any interval 0≤t≤A0\le t\le A0≤t≤A﻿, and both the limit from the right and the limit from the left of fff﻿ exist at every point of discontinuity.

The Laplace transform doesn’t care how the function f(t)f(t)f(t)﻿ behaves when t<0t<0t<0﻿, this means that different functions can have the same Laplace transform as long as they agree on t≥0t\ge 0t≥0﻿.

Properties

In the following, we will assume L{f(t)}=F(s)\mathcal{L}\left\{f(t)\right\}=F(s)L{f(t)}=F(s)﻿, L{g(t)}=G(s)\mathcal{L}\left\{g(t)\right\}=G(s)L{g(t)}=G(s)﻿, or denotes as f(t)⇝F(s)f(t)\rightsquigarrow F(s)f(t)⇝F(s)﻿, g(t)⇝G(s)g(t)\rightsquigarrow G(s)g(t)⇝G(s)﻿. We have the following important properties.

  1. Linearity
  1. Frequency domain derivatives
  1. Time domain derivatives
  1. Frequency shifting
  1. Time shifting

where u(t)u(t)u(t)﻿ is the Heaviside step function (unit step function), and a>0a>0a>0﻿.

  1. Time scaling

where a>0a>0a>0﻿.

  1. Convolution

The convolution between two functions is defined as

then, the Laplace transform of the convolution is the ordinary product of the Laplace transform,

Laplace Transform of Common Functions

The following lists the Laplace transform of some common functions:

  1. Dirac Delta function (unit impulse)
  1. Heaviside step function (unit step)
  1. Polynomial

  1. Exponential decay
  1. Sine and Cosine
  1. Hyperbolic Sine and Cosine

Inverse Laplace Transform

The inversion of the Laplace Transform is given by the Mellin inversion formula. In particular, if fff﻿ is continuous on [0,∞)\left[0,\infty\right)[0,∞)﻿, continuously differentiable on (0,∞)\left(0,\infty\right)(0,∞)﻿, and satisfies the condition for the Laplace transform to exist, then

for all s∈[0,∞)s\in\left[0,\infty\right)s∈[0,∞)﻿, where

is called the Bromwich integral of FFF﻿, and the (positively oriented) contour is defined as

where β∈R\beta\in\Rβ∈R﻿ is any number such that FFF﻿ is analytic for all z∈Cz\in\mathbb{C}z∈C﻿ with Rez≥β\mathrm{Re}z\ge\betaRez≥β﻿.

To calculate the Bromwich integral, we should consider the case when t≥0t\ge0t≥0﻿ and t<0t<0t<0﻿ separately.

For t≥0t\ge 0t≥0﻿, the integration contour is closed on the left.

Let Γ\GammaΓ﻿ be the straight line and Cβ,R\mathcal{C}_{\beta,R}Cβ,R​﻿ be the semicircle (orientation indicated by the arrows). Then, by residue theorem

Letting R→∞R\to \inftyR→∞﻿, the first integral will be the Bromwich integral we want. For the second integral, by applying the substitution rule,

where CR\mathcal{C}_RCR​﻿ is a semi-circle of radius RRR﻿ in the upper half-plane. In most cases, we can use Jordan’s Lemma to show that the right-hand side goes to 0 as R→∞R\to \inftyR→∞﻿.

Then, the Bromwich integral of FFF﻿ is just the sum of all the residues of FFF﻿,

For t<0t<0t<0﻿, the integration contour is closed on the left,

Let Γ\GammaΓ﻿ be the straight line and Cβ,R(2)\mathcal{C}_{\beta,R}^{(2)}Cβ,R(2)​﻿ be the semi-circle (orientation indicated by the arrows). Using a similar argument, we see that

Since the integral over the whole closed contour is zero, and the orientation of the straight line is in the opposite direction, the Bromwich integral is

which in most cases vanishes according to Jordan’s Lemma.

Part 3: Fourier Series and Fourier Transform

In 1807, Joseph Fourier discovered that an arbitrary function f(x)f\left(x\right)f(x)﻿ could be expanded in an infinite series of sines and cosines. This result, though not taken seriously at his time, now ranks as one of the great mathematical theorems of the twentieth century.

Fourier Series

Let fff﻿ and its derivative f′f'f′﻿ be piecewise continuous on an interval −l≤x≤l-l\le x\le l−l≤x≤l﻿. (This means that fff﻿ and f′f'f′﻿ can only have a finite number of discontinuities, and they both have right- and left-hand limits at each point of discontinuity). Compute the numbers

Then, the series, known as the Fourier series,

In other words, one can expand a continuous function fff﻿ defined on an interval [−l,l]\left[-l,l\right][−l,l]﻿ into an infinite linear combination of sine and cosine functions with different frequencies.

It’s clear that the following is true,

  • The Fourier series for an even function is a pure cosine series; i.e., it contains no terms of the form sin⁡nπx/l\sin n\pi x/lsinnπx/l﻿.
  • The Fourier series for an odd function is a pure sine series; i.e., it contains no terms of the form cos⁡nπx/l\cos n\pi x/lcosnπx/l﻿.

No matter whether fff﻿ is even or odd or not, it can always be expanded in either a pure cosine series form

or a pure sine series form

where

Fourier Transform

The Fourier transform of a function f:R→Cf:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{C}f:R→C﻿ is given by

We see that f^\hat{f}f^​﻿ exists for all ξ∈R\xi\in\mathbb{R}ξ∈R﻿ if fff﻿ is absolutely integrable, i.e.,

We define the set Fa\mathcal{F}_aFa​﻿ of functions f:Sa→Cf:S_a\to \mathbb{C}f:Sa​→C﻿, where

such that

  • fff﻿ is analytic on SaS_aSa​﻿
  • there exists a constant A>0A>0A>0﻿ such that for all x∈Rx\in\mathbb{R}x∈R﻿ and ∣y∣<a\left|y\right|<a∣y∣<a﻿,

We denote by F\mathcal{F}F﻿ the set of all complex functions fff﻿ such that f∈Faf\in\mathcal{F}_af∈Fa​﻿ for some a>0a>0a>0﻿. The Fourier inversion theorem states that if f∈Ff\in\mathcal{F}f∈F﻿, then f^\hat{f}f^​﻿ exists, and

for all x∈Rx\in\mathbb{R}x∈R﻿.

This part is mainly about solving the initial value problem

where a,b,ca,b,ca,b,c﻿ are constant.

Basic Method

which is an algebraic equation about YYY﻿. This implies that

Then, the solution is y(t)=L−1{Y(s)}y\left(t\right)=\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{Y\left(s\right)\right\}y(t)=L−1{Y(s)}﻿.

Equations with Discontinuous Input

Using the method of the Laplace transform is very convenient when dealing with a discontinuous function fff﻿ at the right-hand side of the equation.

If fff﻿ has a jump discontinuity, then we can decompose it into the sum of several parts by multiplying with the unit step function

and the unit box function

Part 5: Solving ODEs by Series

Now, we consider a more general case, where we want to solve a linear second-order homogeneous equation with coefficients that are polynomials. Consider the following equation

where p(t)≠0p\left(t\right)\ne 0p(t)=0﻿ in some interval and p(t),q(t),r(t)p\left(t\right),q\left(t\right),r\left(t\right)p(t),q(t),r(t)﻿ are polynomials of ttt﻿.

Properties of Power Series

An infinite series

is called a power series about t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿. The following are some of its properties.

  1. All power series have an interval of convergence. This means that there exists a nonnegative number ρ\rhoρ﻿ such that the infinite series converges for ∣t−t0∣<ρ\left|t-t_0\right|<\rho∣t−t0​∣<ρ﻿, and diverges for ∣t−t0∣>ρ\left|t-t_0\right|>\rho∣t−t0​∣>ρ﻿. The number ρ\rhoρ﻿ is called the radius of convergence of the power series.
  1. The power series can be differentiated and integrated term by term, and the resultant series have the same radius of convergence.
  1. One simple method to determine the radius of convergence of a power series is the Cauchy ratio test. Suppose

Then the radius of convergence is ρ=1λ\displaystyle \rho=\frac{1}{\lambda}ρ=λ1​﻿.

  1. Many functions f(t)f\left(t\right)f(t)﻿ that arise in applications can be expanded in power series, i.e., there exists a series of coefficients a0,a1,a2,…a_0,a_1,a_2,\dotsa0​,a1​,a2​,…﻿ so that

Such functions are said to be analytic at t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿, the series expansion is called the Taylor series of fff﻿ about t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿, and the coefficients can be found by

  1. The radius of convergence can be determined if we treat the function fff﻿ in the complex plane. Let z0∈Cz_0\in\mathbb{C}z0​∈C﻿ be the point that is closest to t0t_0t0​﻿ that f(n)(z0)f^{(n)}(z_0)f(n)(z0​)﻿ fails to exist, then the radius of convergence is ρ=∥z0−t0∥\rho=\left\|z_0-t_0\right\|ρ=∥z0​−t0​∥﻿.
  1. The product of two power series is still a power series, this product is called the Cauchy product

where the coefficient is

which is called the convolution of ana_nan​﻿ and bnb_nbn​﻿.

  1. Important series expansions (Remember them!)

Basic Method

For a second-order equation with variable coefficients (but are analytic functions, at least), although it’s not very rigorous, we can first expect that the solution yyy﻿ is also a power series. Then, by doing a series expansion and setting the polynomial coefficients properly, we can expect the equation to hold. A formal statement of this is as follows.

Let the functions q(t)/p(t)q\left(t\right)/p\left(t\right)q(t)/p(t)﻿ and r(t)/p(t)r\left(t\right)/p\left(t\right)r(t)/p(t)﻿ have convergent Taylor series expansions about t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿, for ∣t−t0∣<ρ\left|t-t_0\right|<\rho∣t−t0​∣<ρ﻿. Then, every solution y(t)y\left(t\right)y(t)﻿ of the differential equation

is analytic at t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿, and the radius of convergence of its Taylor series expansion about t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿ is at least ρ\rhoρ﻿. Set

the coefficients are determined by plugging the expression into the differential equation and setting the sum of the coefficients of like powers of ttt﻿ in this expression equal to zero.

However, it is possible that sometimes p(t0)=0p\left(t_0\right)=0p(t0​)=0﻿, and the above method of series expansion will fail to work, but we still want to find a solution around the point t=t0t=t_0t=t0​﻿. One possible case is Euler’s equation.

Euler’s Equation

The differential equation

where α,β∈R\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}α,β∈R﻿ are constants is known as Euler’s equation.

This equation has a singular point at t=0t=0t=0﻿ , and the series solution method above cannot work. However, we can treat t>0t>0t>0﻿ and t<0t<0t<0﻿ separately.

First, assume that t>0t>0t>0﻿.

For t<0t<0t<0﻿, let t=−xt=-xt=−x﻿ (x>0x>0x>0﻿), and let y=u(x)y=u\left(x\right)y=u(x)﻿. Then the equation is transformed into

which should have the same solution as the case when t>0t>0t>0﻿. Thus, for the final solution, we only need to put a modulus on ttt﻿. Method of Frobenius

Here, we want to find a class of singular differential equations that is more general than Euler’s equation. A very natural generalization is the following

where p(t)p\left(t\right)p(t)﻿ and q(t)q\left(t\right)q(t)﻿ can be expanded in series of the form

Two Complex Roots

If rrr﻿ is a complex root of the indicial equation, then

is a complex-valued solution, whose real and imaginary part are both real-valued solutions.

Equal Roots

If rrr﻿ is the only root of the indicial equation, then we have only one independent solution.

Part 6: Introduction to PDEs

A partial differential equation (PDE) is a relation involving one or more functions of several variables and their partial derivatives. The order of a partial differential equation is the order of the highest partial derivative that appears in the equation.

There are three classical partial differential equations of order two, which are very common in physical applications. They are known as the heat equation (1), the wave equation (2), and the Laplace equation (3).

Heat Equation

Consider the boundary-value problem

First, it’s easy to see that the equation is linear, which means any linear combination of solutions u1(x,t),…,un(x,t)u_1\left(x,t\right),\dots,u_n\left(x,t\right)u1​(x,t),…,un​(x,t)﻿ is again a solution. This suggests the following “plan” for solving this boundary-value problem.

  1. Find as many solutions ui(x,t)u_i\left(x,t\right)ui​(x,t)﻿ as we can that satisfies the boundary condition u(0,t)=u(l,t)=0u\left(0,t\right)=u\left(l,t\right)=0u(0,t)=u(l,t)=0﻿.
  1. Find a proper linear combination of uiu_iui​﻿ to satisfies the initial condition u(x,0)=f(x)u\left(x,0\right)=f\left(x\right)u(x,0)=f(x)﻿.

An important method to solve partial differential equations is the separation of variables, which means we first assume u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)u\left(x,t\right)=X\left(x\right)T\left(t\right)u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)﻿. Then

Plug this into the original equation and get

Divide both sides by α2XT\alpha^2XTα2XT﻿ gives

Notice that the left-hand side only depends on xxx﻿, while the right-hand side only depends on ttt﻿. The only way that they can be equal for any xxx﻿ and ttt﻿ is that both of them are constant. Thus,

By separation of variables, we have transformed a PDE into two solvable ODEs,

Now, plug in the boundary condition.

Since T(t)≠0T\left(t\right)\ne 0T(t)=0﻿ (not identically zero, otherwise uuu﻿ would be identically zero, and we have a trivial solution), we have X(0)=X(l)=0X\left(0\right)=X\left(l\right)=0X(0)=X(l)=0﻿.

Now, we only need to solve

and

The first one is a second-order, linear, constant coefficient ODE, for which we can directly write out the solution. There are three different cases:

  • λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0﻿: The general solution is X(x)=c1x+c2X\left(x\right)=c_1x+c_2X(x)=c1​x+c2​﻿ for some constant c1,c2c_1, c_2c1​,c2​﻿. Plug in the boundary condition, we have c1=c2=0c_1=c_2=0c1​=c2​=0﻿. Thus, there are no non-trivial solution for λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0﻿.
  • λ<0\lambda<0λ<0﻿: The general solution is X(x)=c1e−λx+c1e−λxX\left(x\right)=c_1e^{\sqrt{-\lambda}x}+c_1e^{\sqrt{-\lambda}x}X(x)=c1​e−λ​x+c1​e−λ​x﻿ for some constant c1,c2c_1,c_2c1​,c2​﻿. The boundary condition implies

This linear system of equations has a non-trivial solution c1,c2c_1, c_2c1​,c2​﻿ if and only if

This implies e2−λl=1e^{2\sqrt{-\lambda}l}=1e2−λ​l=1﻿, which is impossible since 2−λl>02\sqrt{-\lambda}l>02−λ​l>0﻿. Therefore, there are also no non-trivial solution for λ<0\lambda < 0λ<0﻿.

  • λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0﻿: The general solution is X(x)=c1cos⁡λx+c2sin⁡λxX\left(x\right)=c_1\cos\sqrt{\lambda}x+c_2\sin\sqrt{\lambda}xX(x)=c1​cosλ​x+c2​sinλ​x﻿ for some constant c1,c2c_1,c_2c1​,c2​﻿ The boundary condition implies
